content: Vaccines work by introducing a harmless piece of a virus or bacteria, such as a protein or sugar, to the body. This triggers the immune system to recognize it as foreign and attack it. As the immune system fights off the invader, it learns how to defeat it and remembers this lesson for future encounters. This way, if the individual is ever exposed to the real virus or bacteria, their immune system is already prepared to defend against it. Think of it like a fire drill - the vaccine is a simulated emergency that allows the immune system to practice its response, so when a real emergency occurs, it knows exactly what to do. This preparation enables the immune system to respond quickly and effectively, often preventing illness or reducing its severity.
